preamble: The transformation from a voiced velar plosive /g/ to a voiceless aspirated velar plosive /kʰ/ is a common phonetic development, typically occurring as a two-stage process involving devoicing and subsequent aspiration.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing of the velar plosive (loss of vocal fold vibration), followed by fortition through aspiration (a puff of air accompanying the release of the stop)

examples.note: Proto-Indo-European *ǵenu- (with a voiced palatovelar stop) underwent the Centum merger (where palatovelars merged with plain velars) and then Grimm's Law, resulting in Proto-Germanic *knewą (with a voiceless velar stop). This voiceless velar stop was subsequently aspirated in word-initial, stressed positions in Old English.

sources: The Evolution of Gothic from Proto-Indo-European | The Germanic Languages.
